excerpt: Intellect is the name of an infinite potential that can transcend the mortal world and reach the spiritual world, encompassing realities there. Reasoning is a finite force moving through logic within mental conceptions.
source_location_hint: epistemology of intellect and reason

excerpt: You will fight for the interpretation of the Qur'an as I fought for the revelation of the Qur'an.
source_location_hint: sermon on knowledge and revelation

excerpt: The multiplicity of all signs of the universe and the soul return to the one truth which is one.
source_location_hint: tawhid and the unity of signs

excerpt: Ali is the true interpreter of the Qur'an, appointed by the Prophet to expound its meanings.
source_location_hint: interpretation and authority

excerpt: The messenger of a man is the interpreter of his intellect.
source_location_hint: intellect and revelation correspondence

excerpt: Through the intellect, outward words produce countless streams of inner meaning, as in the story of Moses striking the stone with his staff whence twelve springs gushed forth.
source_location_hint: intellect as interpreter of revelation

excerpt: No son did Allah beget, nor is there any god along with Him; glorified be Allah above all that they attribute to Him.
source_location_hint: Surah 23:91 on divine oneness

excerpt: Knowledge has charged through them into the realities of insight; they have embraced the spirit of certainty.
source_location_hint: discourse on knowledge and discernment

excerpt: The evils of the soul are like rust upon the heart, obscuring perception.
source_location_hint: spiritual impediments and the soul

excerpt: The Messenger does not speak from desire; it is naught but revelation.
source_location_hint: Surah 53:3-4 on revelation and desire

excerpt: Have you seen him who has taken his own desire as his god?
source_location_hint: Surah 25:43 on desire as false deity

excerpt: He who makes his desire his god and whom Allah leads astray with knowledge—his hearing and heart are sealed.
source_location_hint: Surah 45:23 on spiritual blindness

excerpt: He who fears standing before his Lord and restrains his soul from desire—Paradise is his abode.
source_location_hint: Surah 79:40-41 on restraint and Paradise

excerpt: Think not of those slain in the way of Allah as dead; they are alive with their Lord and sustained.
source_location_hint: Surah 3:169 on martyrdom and life

excerpt: My prayer, sacrifice, life, and death are all for Allah, Lord of the worlds.
source_location_hint: Surah 6:162 on sincerity

excerpt: Worship your Lord until certainty comes to you.
source_location_hint: Surah 15:99 on perpetual devotion

excerpt: the true knowledge of man is knowledge of his own self—a knowledge he cannot fully grasp
source_location_hint: Nahj al-Balagha and associated commentaries

excerpt: Whoever knows his own soul, he knows his Lord
source_location_hint: Prophetic sayings, widely attested

excerpt: Allah is beautiful and loves beautiful things
source_location_hint: Sunan al-Tirmidhi and similar sources

excerpt: The foundation of intellect is love
source_author: Creator/Divine teaching as reported by Ali
source_location_hint: Esoteric exegesis of Qur'anic verses on intellect

excerpt: Know that even the slightest hypocrisy is shirk
source_location_hint: Sermon addressing sincerity and intention

excerpt: My eyes sleep, but my heart remains awake
source_location_hint: attested in Sunan collections

excerpt: Allah keeps His servants in the state they desire
source_location_hint: attested in collections of prophetic sayings

excerpt: Death is as dear to me as a mother's breast to an infant
source_location_hint: sermon expressing detachment from worldly life

excerpt: I am death's death
source_location_hint: expressing transcendence of fear and worldly attachment

excerpt: This world is like a serpent—smooth to the touch, yet filled with deadly venom; To me, this world is like the substance that flows from a goat's nose
source_location_hint: sermons on contempt for worldliness

excerpt: His contempt for the world's trivialities made him great in my eyes
source_location_hint: sermon on asceticism and spiritual greatness

excerpt: Even if the veil were lifted, my certainty would not increase
source_location_hint: expressing the permanence of gnostic certainty

excerpt: Justice is the placement of things in their proper stations
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha / Letter to Malik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: letter on governance and justice

excerpt: Tell me about excellence (ihsan)
source_work: hadith / Gabriel's questions to the Prophet
source_location_hint: Sahih Muslim and similar collections (hadith al-Jibrīl)

excerpt: Every deed you perform depends upon your intention—upon how you regard goodness
source_location_hint: sermon on intention and sincerity in deeds

excerpt: Pretense, arrogance, and self-magnification are Satan's finest snares, through which he destroys others
source_location_hint: sermon on vices and spiritual pitfalls

excerpt: When a ruler becomes arrogant, he usurps one of the supreme attributes reserved for the Divine alone, forgetting that al-Mutakabbir, the Mighty One, alone possesses this title by absolute right.
source_location_hint: Letter to Malik al-Ashtar

excerpt: The ruler's notion of superiority over his subjects becomes, in fact, a declaration of rebellion against the One who stands above him.
source_location_hint: Discussion of arrogance and rebellion

excerpt: Iblis became the first to confront Allah in the realm of pride, thereby establishing himself as the source and exemplar of all subsequent arrogance, and assumed the station of leader of all the fanatical and the haughty.
source_location_hint: Sermon on arrogance and Iblis

excerpt: The Prophet taught: 'Shirk is hidden in my Community like the creeping of a black ant upon a black stone in a black night.'
source_work: Sunan al-Tirmidhi and other hadith collections
source_location_hint: Hadith on hidden shirk

excerpt: Ali instructed Malik al-Ashtar: Do not say, 'I possess authority; I command and am obeyed,' for such a notion corrupts the heart and ruins religion.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53 to Malik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: Letter on governance

excerpt: When your position breeds arrogance and display, contemplate the magnificence of Allah, above whom you stand under His dominion.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53 to Malik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: Counsel to Malik al-Ashtar

excerpt: Do not say, 'I command, and obey me,' for such is the path of the soul commanding to evil—that which commands the body, imagination, intellect, intention, emotion, and consciousness.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53 to Malik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: On the commanding soul

excerpt: I have appointed you guardian of the people of Egypt. It devolves upon you by necessity to oppose your own self and protect your religion!
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr
source_location_hint: Letter to Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr, Governor of Egypt

excerpt: In your judgment, your own actions should be the most worthy, most equitable, and most acceptable to people—yet do not allow people's disapproval to weaken your approval.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53 to Malik al-Ashtar
source_location_hint: On self-respect and approval

excerpt: A family of ascetics complained they were living in unnecessary poverty. One replied: O Ali! You counsel us thus, and you yourself wrap yourself in a rough woolen garment and sustain yourself on dry bread!
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha and Islamic historical sources
source_location_hint: Incident with ascetics

excerpt: Acts of mercy draw Allah's mercy upon you. You will be shown mercy according as you show mercy to others. I marvel at one who expects mercy from above yet shows none to those below!
source_location_hint: On mercy

excerpt: By Allah, with complete sincerity—if it reaches me that you have misappropriated the wealth of the Muslims, whether little or much, I will inflict upon you a punishment that will diminish your riches, break your back, and bring ruin to your deeds!
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter to Governor of Basra
source_location_hint: Letter on embezzlement

excerpt: When Ali's brother Aqeel requested an additional stipend from the treasury, Ali placed a burning iron bar in his hand. Aqeel shrieked. Ali said: Brother Aqeel, if you cannot endure this brief fire, how shall I bear the blazing fire of Allah's Hell on the Day of Resurrection—a fire to which you invite me?
source_location_hint: Incident with Aqeel

excerpt: O Allah, I do not worship You through longing for Your Paradise, nor through fear of Your Fire, but I have found You worthy of worship, therefore I worship You.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Sermon on intentions and sincere worship

excerpt: Whoever fears Allah, Allah shall make for him a way out from trials and light in darkness.
source_location_hint: On piety and guidance through trials

excerpt: My servant draws not near to Me with anything more beloved to Me than the obligatory duties I have imposed upon him. My servant continues to draw near to Me through supererogatory deeds until I love him. When I love him, I am his hearing by which he hears, his sight by which he sees, his hand by which he grasps, and his feet by which he walks.
source_location_hint: On nearness to Allah through worship

excerpt: Verily, Allah has set remembrance in hearts, through which they may hear when deaf and see when blind.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: On the function of dhikr in human perception

excerpt: Indeed, there are those who have adopted the remembrance of Allah in such a way that neither commerce nor livelihood diverts them from His remembrance...Spirituality is not separate from action; it is a connection to the moral depths that arise from the relationship itself.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: On the integration of dhikr and action

excerpt: Had I not seen him, I would not have worshipped him...He cannot be seen with the eyes of the head, but the heart sees him with the truth of faith.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: On spiritual vision and the heart's knowledge

excerpt: One of them was the cousin of Muhammad Mustafa (peace be upon him), who was drowned in the stormy sea of trials, and who was engulfed by the flames of love. They have a grand and noble place in this path.
source_location_hint: early section on ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib

excerpt: The word dhikr is very eloquent in its meaning and has no substitute for it in other languages. It is taken in the sense of remembrance, reflection, complaint, repetition, tasbih, recitation. Remembrance is the power by which a person preserves knowledge and knowledge.
source_location_hint: semantic analysis of dhikr

excerpt: There are numerous hadiths of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allaah be upon him) that describe the virtue of the remembrance of Allah. Once he said to his companions, 'Shall I not tell you what is the purest of your deeds?' Similarly, Maulana Ali says, 'Remember Allah continuously, for He enlightens the hearts.'
source_author: Prophet Muhammad and ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: exhortations to remembrance

excerpt: Indeed, Allah has made the remembrance of Allah a polish for the hearts, with which they may hear when they are deaf, see when they are blind, and submit when they resist. In every age, there have been servants of Allah to whom Allah has spoken through the origin of their thoughts and their intellects.
source_location_hint: passage on the polish of remembrance

excerpt: Maulana Ali explained about this hidden consciousness in depths, which reveals the truths to him. So it became clear that the real purpose of dhikr is the purification of the heart, which is the Amir al-Badan and the center of true insight.
source_location_hint: on the heart as center of cognition

excerpt: The Messenger of Allah (s.a.w.s.) has described such people as the people of remembrance. He said, 'There are angels of Allaah who travel on the streets in search of the people of remembrance, and when they meet them, they say to one another, 'Come to that which you desire.'
source_location_hint: on the excellence of dhikr-gatherings

excerpt: Abu Talib bin Makki (a Sufi) writes that if it is true that Allah enters into human feelings through human actions, then the highest level of spiritual consciousness would be that the vision of Allah is not the sight of Allah, but the sight of Allah becomes the sight of Allah.
source_work: spiritual philosophy cited in commentary tradition
source_location_hint: on the transformation of vision

excerpt: Maulana Ali in his first sermon says, 'The first of religion is its knowledge.' The first means the former, which can be taken in the sense of the beginning, the foundation. The meaning of knowledge is not only to acquire knowledge, but also to recognize.
source_location_hint: opening of first sermon

excerpt: In the same sermon, he says: 'Allah has sent Messengers from among them to take a pledge from them, and to remind them of the blessings which they have forgotten...to bring forth for them the hidden treasures of intellect.'
source_location_hint: first sermon on divine guidance

excerpt: Spiritual inspiration enables a person to see seemingly different objects as one object in the light of monotheism. At this deep level of consciousness, the process of reconstruction begins and from it begins the knowledge of the Truth and the heart begins to see Allah through the sight of the truth of faith.
source_location_hint: on the unification of opposites

excerpt: The Names of God are not invented by human convention but are the self-disclosure of the Divine through which He is accessible to creation.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the Divine Names and their relationship to the Divinity

excerpt: He who loves something mentions it with passion—the tongue becomes devoted to what the heart cherishes.
source_location_hint: Maxims on the relationship between love and remembrance

excerpt: The Name of God serves as a cure for the disease of human error and the negligence that has become second nature to fallen humanity.
source_location_hint: Section on the Names of God as remedies for spiritual sickness

excerpt: The Imams of the Household of the Prophet integrated remembrance into all moments of life, engaging in dhikr while eating and walking, demonstrating that remembrance transcends formal ritual.
source_location_hint: Accounts of the devotional practices of the Imams

excerpt: the heart which is the mirror of inward nature, the nursery from which all spiritual virtues are born
source_work: various sermonic teachings on the nature of the heart in remembrance
source_location_hint: general dhikr doctrine

excerpt: primary perfection and secondary perfection as stages of spiritual attainment
source_location_hint: discourse on spiritual stations and perfections

excerpt: My servant ceases not to draw nigh unto Me through the performance of the supererogatory devotions until I love him
source_work: Kitab al-Yanabiʿ; scattered references in Ismaili hadith collections
source_location_hint: on intimacy and divine love

excerpt: Excellence is that you worship Allah as if you see Him, and if you do not see Him, know that He sees you
source_authority: hadith of Prophet Muhammad, transmitted in Ismaili literature
source_location_hint: on the stations of worship and ihsan

excerpt: There have come upon you nights and days of knowledge of perfect tawhīd that no angel or prophet can bear
source_location_hint: on the state of unity beyond angelic comprehension

excerpt: Every servant has a name derived from his Master. Thus the servant becomes the body of the Master, and the Master becomes the heart of the servant
source_work: Futuhat al-Makkiyya; Islamic mystical philosophy adapted in Ismaili thought
source_author: Ibn al-ʿArabi; echoed in Ismaili exegesis
source_location_hint: on the Names and their manifestation in the heart

excerpt: I have drawn the sword for Allah's sake... I am the Lion of Allah... I do not slay men in war—I give them life in the Hereafter
source_work: Masnavi; Ismaili sermonic parallels in Nahj al-Balagha commentary
source_author: Jalal al-Din Rumi; references to Ali's sayings
source_authority: mystical poetry and Ismaili doctrinal literature
source_location_hint: on annihilation in the Divine and spiritual warfare

excerpt: dhikr al-lisan, dhikr al-qalb, dhikr al-sirr, dhikr al-ruh, dhikr al-khafi—five stations of remembrance
source_work: al-Iftikhar; Kashf al-Mahjub; Ismaili mystical treatises
source_location_hint: on the gradations of remembrance and unveiling

excerpt: The Name of Allah is distinct from His Essence... Allah is He to whom the Names point; they are distinct from Him and from His creation
source_location_hint: on the distinction and unity of Names and Essence

excerpt: The perfection of sincerity is to purify Allah of all attributes, because every attribute testifies to the one to whom it is attributed
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi, compiler of Maulana Ali's words
source_location_hint: on the negation of attributes in true tawhīd

excerpt: On the night of the Mi'raj, I learned the true significance of the Names, and I saw them return to their object and reality
source_work: Jamiʿ al-Hikmatayn; Ismaili commentary on the Ascension
source_author: Nasir-i Khusraw; echoed in Ismaili doctrinal works
source_authority: mystical exegesis of the Mi'raj tradition
source_location_hint: on the Prophet's knowledge of the divine Names

excerpt: I have completely purged my soul of arrogance. In its place has come the majesty of Allah... the dust of ego and desire from the mirror of my heart
source_authority: Imami hadith tradition within Ismaili framework
source_location_hint: on the replacement of ego with divine attributes

excerpt: the person reaches the position of Arif billah (one who knows through Allah), whereas otherwise he remains in ilm billah (knowledge of Allah)
source_author: Jaʿfar ibn Mansur al-Yaman; Ismaili mystical tradition
source_location_hint: on the stations of knowing Allah

excerpt: The most mysterious death is that in which the tongue remains moist with the remembrance of Allah
source_work: Various Ismaili hadith collections; spiritual teachings on death
source_author: Prophet Muhammad; transmitted through Ismaili sources
source_location_hint: on the ideal death of a believer

excerpt: Intellect is that which is capable of a direct contemplative vision of transcendent realities. Reason, by contrast, operates indirectly and discursively.
source_location_hint: On the distinction between intellect and reason in mystical knowledge

excerpt: Maulana Ali's life and teachings centered upon the Qur'anic revelation and its embodiment in the Prophet's conduct, which can be read as a spiritual commentary on the sacred text.
source_location_hint: On the taʾwīl of revelation in the life of Imam Ali

excerpt: Ali will fight for the taʾwīl of the Qur'an as I fought for its tanzīl.
source_location_hint: Saying of the Prophet concerning Imam Ali's role in esoteric interpretation

excerpt: He is the First and the Last, the Outward and the Inward. And He is the All-Knower of every thing.
source_location_hint: Surah al-Hadid, verse 3

excerpt: We will show them Our Signs in the universe, and in their ownselves, until it becomes manifest to them that this is the truth.
source_location_hint: Surah Fussilat, verse 53

excerpt: Truly, Ali is with the Qur'an, and the Qur'an is with Ali.
source_location_hint: Saying of the Prophet concerning the inseparability of Imam Ali from the Qur'anic message

excerpt: The dialectical interplay between form and formlessness, the evident and the hidden, the revealed and the concealed lies at the root of the paradoxical mode of expression for which Maulana Ali is renowned.
source_location_hint: On paradox and antinomy in Imam Ali's discourse

excerpt: One purpose of Allah's sending forth of prophets to mankind was to unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects (dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl).
source_location_hint: Opening sermon describing divine revelation and the awakening of intellectual capacities

excerpt: Eyes see Him not through sight's observation, but hearts see Him through the true principles and beliefs of faith.
source_location_hint: On the heart's spiritual vision of the Divine

excerpt: The shock of apparent contradiction precipitates a leap of consciousness from the formal activity of reason to the super-rational contemplation of the spirit.
source_location_hint: On the function of paradox in mystical transformation

excerpt: One can place each negated attribute in the place of the negation (nafi) of the first testimony, arriving at formulations such as: Lā Raḥmāna illā Allāh, There is no All-Compassionate except Allah.
source_location_hint: On the dialectic of negation and affirmation in tawhīd

excerpt: Allah has not begotten a son, nor is there any god besides Him; if there were, each god would have taken away what he had created, and some of them would have risen above others. Glory be to Allah, that which they describe.
source_location_hint: Surah al-Mu'minun, verse 91

excerpt: The remembrance of Allah (Dhikr Allāh) is the most excellent form of worship.
source_location_hint: On the pre-eminence of divine remembrance among devotional practices

excerpt: Then He fashioned him in due proportion, and breathed into him the soul, and He gave you hearing, sight and hearts. Little is the thanks you give!
source_location_hint: Surah al-Sajdah, verse 9, on the inbreathing of the divine spirit

excerpt: O you who believe! Answer Allah and (His) Messenger when he calls you to that which will give you life, and know that Allah comes in between a person and his heart. And verily to Him you shall (all) be gathered.
source_location_hint: Surah al-Anfal, verse 24, on the life-giving call to divine remembrance
